translations.descricao: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, associated with an increased risk of stroke (CVA) and heart failure. Opportunistic screening is essential for early detection in at-risk populations, reducing thromboembolic complications. The global prevalence is estimated at 2-3% in adults, increasing to 8-10% in elderly people over 80 years. In Brazil, the prevalence is approximately 1.4% in the general population, with a greater impact on the elderly.
translations.sus.indicacao: Screening recommended for individuals ≥65 years and those with cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes, family history).
translations.sus.populacaoAlvo: Adults ≥65 years; adults <65 years with risk factors such as arterial hypertension, diabetes mellitus or heart disease.
translations.sociedadesMedicas.indicacao: The European Society of Cardiology (ESC 2020) and American Heart Association (AHA 2019) recommend systematic screening in adults ≥65 years for stroke prevention.
translations.epidemiologia.prevalencia: The global prevalence of AF is 2.7% in adults (about 33 million cases), with exponential increase with age. In Brazil, it is estimated at 1.4% in the adult population, affecting about 2.3 million people.
translations.epidemiologia.incidencia: The global incidence is 0.6-1.0 cases per 100 person-years, rising to 2% in the elderly. In Brazil, the annual incidence is approximately 0.2-0.5 per 100 inhabitants.
translations.epidemiologia.mortalidade: AF contributes to 20-30% of ischemic stroke cases, with mortality 1.5-2 times higher in affected patients. Globally, it is associated with 113,000 annual deaths; in Brazil, it is a factor in about 10% of cardiovascular deaths.
